description:
GOAL: Wire heap-allocated closures through the CPS pipeline so higher-order code (`map(f, [h|t])`, `reduce`, fn args to spawn, etc.) actually runs via JIT. First non-trivial heap struct to flow through `Term::CallClosure` and `Term::TailCallClosure`.

## Why this is a separate ticket

Filed alongside `.11.10` (Lists end-to-end). The ticket's `map` test was deferred because closure invocation requires a heap-typed callable carrying `[fn_id, ...captured_vars]` — overlap with `.11.11` (general struct heap allocation). Splitting keeps `.11.10` focused on list-as-value semantics; this ticket owns the higher-order story end-to-end.

## Decisions captured

- Closure layout: `HeapHeader (16) + fn_id: u32 + arity: u16 + n_captured: u16 + captured: [FzValue; n]`. Header.kind = `HeapKind::Struct`; schema_id points to a per-fn closure schema registered at compile time. (Or: a dedicated `HeapKind::Closure` to skip schema lookup — decide during implementation; doesn't affect external behavior.)
- Closure construction: `Prim::MakeClosure(fn_id, captured)` allocates the heap object (already lowered in ir_lower).
- Closure invocation (`Term::CallClosure { closure_var, args, continuation }`): load fn_id, allocate callee frame, splice `[...captured, ...args]` into entry-param slots, dispatch via per-fn-id ptr table.
- Tail variant `Term::TailCallClosure` reuses the frame-reuse logic from `.11.8` when the closure's fn shares the caller's schema; falls back to fresh-frame alloc otherwise.
- Closures participate in GC: traced as Struct via the schema, so captured FzValues get marked.

## Deliverables

- src/ir_codegen.rs:
  - `Prim::MakeClosure` lowering: call `fz_alloc_closure(fn_id, ...captured_bits)` runtime fn.
  - `Term::CallClosure` lowering: load fn_id from closure ptr, allocate callee frame, write captured + args into slots, set up continuation frame as in `Term::Call`.
  - `Term::TailCallClosure` lowering: same-fn frame reuse OR fresh alloc.
  - Runtime fn `fz_alloc_closure` (variadic via fixed max-captured cap, OR a thunk that reads from a pre-staged buffer).
  - GC tracer integration: closures' captured slots traced (already works if implemented as Struct + schema).
- Schema registration: each fn that's the target of `MakeClosure` gets a closure schema registered alongside its frame schema during `compile()`.

## Tests

- `fn double(x), do: x * 2; fn apply(f, n), do: f(n); fn main(), do: apply(double, 21)` -> 42 via JIT (simple closure, no captures).
- `fn make_adder(k), do: fn(x) -> x + k end; fn main(), do: (make_adder(10))(5)` -> 15 (closure with capture).
- `fn map(_, []), do: []; fn map(f, [h|t]), do: [f(h) | map(f, t)]; fn main(), do: print(map(double, [1, 2, 3]))` renders `[2, 4, 6]` (heap-allocated higher-order list traversal — the test originally listed under .11.10).
- Closure with capture allocated, dropped, GC frees both the closure and its captured cons-cell tail.
- TailCallClosure reuses frame when target fn shares caller's schema (count-via-closure stays bounded over 100k iterations).

## Out of scope

- Tuples / general user structs (.11.11).
- Closures over mutable cells (no mutation in fz; not applicable).
- Polymorphic dispatch / multi-clause closures with guards (handled by lowering, not codegen — already supported in ir_lower).
- Optimizing flat captures (escape analysis to stack-allocate non-escaping closures).

## Dependencies

- BLOCKED BY: fz-ul4.11.11 (Tuples as struct schema). Closures are structs — landing the general struct path first means this ticket's MakeClosure / CallClosure can reuse `Prim::AllocStruct` codegen + the schema-driven trace path rather than inventing closure-specific allocator logic. If `.11.11` ends up not actually wiring AllocStruct (e.g., it does tuples a different way), revisit this dependency.

## Notes on the deferral

- Authorized 2026-05-10 during `.11.10` landing: 3 of 4 ticket tests landed in `.11.10` (lists-as-values + GC). The `map` test was deferred here because closures are a separate concern. Lifting the gated tests in `src/{codegen,jit,aot}.rs` that exercised higher-order traversal (e.g., `map(double, ...)`) is part of this ticket's scope.

comments.text:
Implementation notes:
- Repr: HeapKind::Closure (dedicated kind, ticket-authorized alternative). header.schema_id = fn_id; header.flags = captured count (u16). No per-fn closure schema needed.
- Runtime: TLS-staged builder (begin/push/finalize) for MakeClosure; TLS-staged args (arg) + invoke/tail_closure for CallClosure/TailCallClosure.
- fz_alloc_frame_dyn looks up frame size via FRAME_SIZES TLS populated at compile() time.
- Frame reuse in tail-call path triggers when closure.fn_id == caller_frame.schema_id.
- ir_lower change: bare top-level fn name in expression position lowers to MakeClosure(fn_id, []) (0-captured); first defined arity wins.
- Tests: 5 in ir_codegen (apply/no-cap, captures-local, map-higher-order, GC trace, tail-loop 100k iters) + 2 in heap (closure trace, closure unrooted-frees).
- Legacy gated tests in src/{codegen,jit,aot}.rs not literally un-gated: those exercised the retired monomorphize-and-JIT pipeline (specialization, tier-up) whose architecture no longer exists. The HOF coverage they intended (map(double, ...)) is now in ir_codegen::map_higher_order_renders_doubled_list.

title: Closures end-to-end via CPS
